    Home › Aircraft › Douglas DC-8

    Douglas DC-8

    Douglas · Narrowbody · Retired (a handful of specials)

    The other first jetliner.

    Length57.12 m
    Wingspan45.23 m
    Range7,400 km
    MTOW159 t
    Typical seats259
    Built556
    Our numbers

    Derived metrics

    Computed by aircraft.fyi from the core specification — see how we calculate these.

    Seats per metre#14 of 30
    4.53 seats/m typical seats ÷ fuselage length
    Range per tonne#17 of 40
    46.6 km/t maximum range ÷ MTOW in tonnes
    Span-to-length ratio#38 of 43
    0.79 wingspan ÷ fuselage length
    Weight per metre#32 of 43
    2.78 t/m MTOW ÷ fuselage length
    Range in hours#26 of 40
    7.7 h maximum range ÷ cruise speed
    Weight per seat#22 of 30
    613 kg MTOW ÷ typical seats
    Sense of scale

    How big is it, really?

    The Douglas DC-8's wingspan, against things you already know the size of.

    Douglas DC-845.23 m
    Football pitch (width)68 m
    Blue whale25 m
    New Routemaster bus11.2 m
    You (probably)1.75 m
    #22 of 43 by length → #32 by weight → #7 by speed →
    Specification

    The numbers

    Length (Super 61/63)57.12 m
    Wingspan (Super 63)45.23 m
    Height13.11 m
    MTOW (Super 63)158,757 kg
    Typical seats (Super 61)up to 259
    Engines4 × JT3D (CFM56 on Super 70s)
    First flight30 May 1958
    Built556

    Compare it

    Put the Douglas DC-8 next to something

    vs Boeing 757 →vs Ilyushin Il-62 →
    vs Boeing 757 vs DC-10 Compare with anything →
    ← PreviousDe Havilland Canada Dash 8-400 Next →Embraer E-Jet E2

    Last verified: 2026-07-14 · Spot an error? business@luck.fyi

    The story

    What makes it different

    The DC-8 lost the sales war to the 707 and won almost everything else. In August 1961 one was put into a shallow dive over Edwards and hit Mach 1.012 — the first airliner, and still one of very few, ever to go supersonic. And where 707s were largely scrap by the 1980s, DC-8s kept flying: the Super 60 stretches and later CFM56 re-engining made them durable freighters into the 2000s.

    The Super 61 and 63, at 57.12 metres, were the longest airliners in the world until the 747 — a slim tube carrying 259 passengers, the true prototype of the stretched narrowbody.

    NASA flew one as a flying laboratory until the 2020s, and Samaritan's Purse still operates a DC-8-72 on relief missions — a 1960s jet still earning its keep.

    Variants

    The Douglas DC-8 line

    DC-8-10 to -50
    The original length, 1958–60s.
    Super 60 series
    The 57.12 m stretches — longest airliners of their day.
    Super 70 series
    CFM56 re-engined — the long-lived freighters.
    Operators

    Who flies the Douglas DC-8

    All 1 current operators, verified 2026-07-14. Fleet counts are approximate where marked ≈.

    USSamaritan's PurseA DC-8-72 flying humanitarian relief — the type's last regular operator.1

    About the data. Every specification is compiled from manufacturer documents and cited per row. Operator fleet counts are approximate snapshots, marked ≈ and dated; individually verified figures carry a ✓. All silhouettes are original works of aircraft.fyi, drawn to true scale. Corrections and enquiries: business@luck.fyi — we fix errors visibly, not silently.
